category: Large Polyp Stony (LPS) Coral — solitary disc polyp; formerly Scolymia australis (reclassified 2014); premium trade item

nativeRange.habitat: Shallow reefs and reef slopes with low to moderate flow; most commonly collected from the shallow waters surrounding Australia and the Great Barrier Reef; tends to inhabit lower-relief, somewhat turbid reef zones rather than high-current crests

decorPreferences: angled substrate to aid detritus shedding
at least 15 cm spacing from neighboring corals
stable positioning — avoid areas with tumbling flow
live rock in vicinity for water quality

diet.notes: Derives nutrition primarily from symbiotic zooxanthellae (photosynthesis) and benefits significantly from supplemental target-feeding. Spot-feeding with meaty foods — mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, krill, chopped marine fish, or small chunks of seafood — weekly is generally recommended. The coral extends feeding tentacles in the evening hours, making that the optimal time to deliver food directly onto the oral disc via pipette or turkey baster. Avoid overfeeding; uneaten food should be removed to prevent water quality issues. Stable low-to-moderate nitrate (5-10 ppm) and phosphate (0.05-0.1 ppm) appear to benefit long-term health.

goodWith: peaceful LPS corals placed at least 15 cm away
avoidWith: corals placed within sweeper tentacle range at night
aggressive LPS corals (hammer, torch, frogspawn) without generous spacing
predatory fish that may nip at the fleshy polyp (triggers, pufferfish, large wrasses)
high direct flow that prevents polyp expansion or damages tissue against septa
other solitary disc LPS corals placed immediately adjacent

summary: Homophyllia australis is a large solitary LPS disc coral (formerly Scolymia australis) prized for vivid multi-color morphs. Best placed on the sand bed under low light and gentle flow with weekly meaty feedings.
careNotes:
Homophyllia australis, widely known in the reef hobby as the 'Australian Scoly' or simply 'Scoly,' is one of the most visually striking and coveted large polyp stony (LPS) corals in the marine aquarium trade. Its large, fleshy round polyp — typically 5–13 cm in diameter — can expand to roughly twice its contracted size during the day, displaying extraordinary color patterns ranging from solid greens, reds, and oranges to intricate 'Master Scoly' tie-dyed morphs with four or more distinct colors. Premium specimens have historically sold for hundreds to several thousand dollars.

Taxonomic note — 2014 reclassification: This species was known as Scolymia australis (Milne Edwards & Haime, 1848) for most of its hobbyist history. Molecular phylogenetic work in the early 2010s revealed that the Pacific 'scolymia' familiar to aquarists was in fact a very distant relative of the true Atlantic Scolymia genus (family Mussidae). The skeletal septa of the Pacific species are blunted and hillocked, contrasting with the finer, more cylindrical spines of true Atlantic Scolymia. The Pacific species was consequently transferred to the resurrected genus Homophyllia (family Lobophylliidae) — a reclassification widely accepted by WoRMS and the aquarium trade by approximately 2014. The trade name 'Scolymia' and 'Scoly' remain in widespread use despite the formal reclassification.

Sibling taxon — Caribbean Scolymia: See also [scolymia-coral] (sw-coral-057), which covers the true Atlantic Scolymia species (family Mussidae), distinct in both genetics and skeletal morphology. Caribbean Scolymia are rarely seen in the trade; the vast majority of 'Scolymia' available in aquarium stores are Australian Homophyllia australis.

Key care requirements:

• Lighting: Generally advised to keep under low to moderate PAR (30–100). As a sand-bed dweller from lower-light reef zones, excessive light tends to cause bleaching. Sources range from 75–150 PAR, but the lower end of that range (30–100) is more consistent with the species' natural habitat and long-term aquarium success. Acclimate new specimens gradually.

• Flow: Low flow is strongly recommended. Direct or high-velocity flow can smash delicate tissue against the skeletal septa, causing tearing. Indirect, gentle, turbulent flow — just enough to prevent detritus from settling on the polyp — is the general guidance. Angling the coral slightly on the substrate can assist in shedding waste.

• Placement: Best placed on the sand bed or low-lying rubble in the lower third of the aquarium. This matches the species' natural habitat on reef slopes and allows it to face upward for even light distribution.

• Aggression: Has been known to extend sweeper tentacles nightly to deter neighbors. Maintain at least 15 cm clearance from neighboring corals. The species has relatively short sweeper tentacles compared to some LPS (such as hammer or torch corals), but the fleshy expanded disc itself can occupy significant lateral space when fully inflated.

• Feeding: Weekly spot-feeding with meaty foods is generally recommended for long-term health. Feed in the evening when feeding tentacles are extended; use a pipette or turkey baster to place food directly onto the oral disc. The large gape allows consumption of surprisingly large prey items.

• Fragging: Fragging success rate is significantly lower than for most other LPS corals, and permanent damage is a real risk. Propagation for the hobby is predominantly via wild collection from Australia. This contributes to the coral's typically high price.

• Care level note: Sources disagree — LiveAquaria rates it 'Easy,' Reeflex rates it 'Advanced,' and ReefChasers 'Beginner.' Intermediate is adopted here, reflecting the consensus of most reef hobbyist communities: the species is forgiving of moderate parameter variation compared to SPS, but requires stable water chemistry, weekly target feeding, and correct low-light/low-flow placement — not appropriate for an absolute beginner's first reef.

• Color and pricing: 'Master Scoly' or 'Ultra Scoly' specimens with four or more distinct color zones, intricate patterns, or the extremely rare orange morph command premium prices ($500–$5,000+). Standard red/green specimens are more accessible.
breedingNotes: Reproduction in the aquarium is not commonly observed. In the wild, Homophyllia australis reproduces sexually via broadcast spawning. Captive fragging carries a high risk of permanent damage due to the single-polyp solitary structure and sensitive tissue; it is generally not recommended for this species. Commercial propagation for the trade is almost exclusively through wild collection from Australian waters.

primary.name: LiveAquaria — Australian Scolymia Coral, Red
primary.notes: Primary source: temperature 72–78°F (22–25.6°C), salinity 1.023–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, kH 8–12, care level Easy, placement Bottom, flow Medium, feeding meaty foods. Family listed on page as Mussidae; corrected to Lobophylliidae per WoRMS and modern molecular taxonomy.

additional.name: ReefBuilders — Introduction to Scolymia (Homophyllia australis) Care
additional.notes: Cross-checked: temperature 76–78°F, salinity 1.025–1.026, kH 8–12, calcium 400–500 ppm, magnesium 1250–1350 ppm; confirmed low-to-moderate lighting, indirect flow, sand-bed placement, weekly meaty feeding. Documents the reclassification from Scolymia australis to Homophyllia australis.

additional.name: ReefChasers — Scolymia Coral Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked: temperature 77–84°F (25–29°C), PAR 75–125 (successfully kept to 200 with acclimation), kH 8–9, calcium 400–450 ppm, placement bottom, low-to-moderate flow, care level Beginner, nightly sweeper tentacles, 'at least 3 inches' spacing.

additional.name: AquariumSource — Scoly Coral Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked: adult size 3–4 inches (7.6–10 cm), temperature 73–84°F (22.8–28.9°C), salinity 1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, kH 8–12, calcium 400 ppm, magnesium 1350 ppm, PAR 100–150, minimum tank 10 gal. Taxonomy listed as Mussidae/Scolymia (outdated); corrected to Lobophylliidae/Homophyllia.

additional.name: Reeflex.net — Homophyllia australis
additional.notes: Cross-checked: adult size up to 15 cm, depth range 0–40 m, native range (Australia, Indonesia, Philippines, Papua New Guinea, Japan, Malaysia, Singapore, Thailand, Palau, Micronesia, Guam), minimum tank 33 gal (~150 L), care level Advanced, family Lobophylliidae confirmed, moderate flow.

additional.name: WoRMS — Homophyllia australis
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy confirmation (Family Lobophylliidae, Order Scleractinia) and synonym list (Scolymia australis, Caryophyllia australis, Isophyllia australis, Parascolymia australis). Original description: Milne Edwards & Haime, 1848–1849.
